acholuria: A- 'without' plus chol- 'bile' plus -uria 'in the urine.' Eighteenth-century clinicians already knew that a jaundiced patient with pale urine had a fundamentally different disease from one with tea-brown urine.
anuria: Simply the alpha-privative on the Greek for urine.
balanitis: The Greek anatomists thought the tip of the male organ looked like an acorn.
bordetella: Named for the Belgian bacteriologist Jules Bordet. His Nobel came for antibody complement, not for the cough he cultured.
bronchiectasis: Laennec, the inventor of the stethoscope, described the permanent airway dilations at autopsy in 1819 and named them from the Greek.
burns: Ambroise Paré, the sixteenth-century French army surgeon, replaced the hot-oil treatment with a gentler poultice and changed the field forever.
cachexia: Hippocrates used the Greek word — literally 'bad condition' — for the wasting of chronic disease.
catatonia: The nineteenth-century German psychiatrist Kahlbaum described this 'tension insanity' in 1874. His name for it held; his theories on it did not.
cheilosis: Persian and Byzantine physicians described the fissuring at the corners of the mouth that comes with a particular deficiency; the Greek word for lip supplies the stem.
choledocholithiasis: A stone lodges where two systems share plumbing. Cholé (bile), doché (receptacle), lithos (stone), iasis (condition) — Greek all the way down.
cholecystectomy: The first was performed by Carl Langenbuch in Berlin in 1882. The laparoscopic version came a century later.
choroiditis: First cleanly seen with the ophthalmoscope Helmholtz invented in 1851.
colitis: In the American Civil War, it killed more soldiers than the rifles did.
crigler: Two American pediatricians described the enzyme deficiency in 1952 — a rare hereditary jaundice.
dacryoadenitis: Greek dakryon 'tear' plus adeno 'gland' — the paired gland just above the outer eye that makes them.
dislocation: Hippocrates wrote three treatises on it. His method for reducing the shoulder — foot in the axilla, pull — still bears his name.
dissociation: Pierre Janet's late-nineteenth-century Paris lectures separated this psychiatric umbrella term from what Freud would soon rebrand as repression.
dupuytrens: Napoleon's chief surgeon lent his name to the palmar contracture that gradually curls the ring and little digits inward.
dysgeusia: From Greek dys- 'bad' plus geusis 'taste.' A distorted flavor from chemotherapy or zinc deficiency.
dysmenorrhea: Greek dys- plus menorrhea 'monthly flow.' Prostaglandins peak in the first two days; NSAIDs quiet them.
dysuria: Greek dys- 'bad' plus -uria 'urination.' A three-day course of antibiotics resolves most cases in an otherwise healthy woman.
ecchymosis: The Greek ek- 'out' plus chymos 'juice.' The color chart of a bruise, in one word.
ecthyma: Greek 'to erupt.' A deeper, punched-out version of an ordinary impetigo.
epiglottitis: A child sits forward and drools. Once caused mostly by Haemophilus influenzae type B; the vaccine changed the epidemiology in the 1990s.
erysipelas: The Greek word for 'red skin.' Hippocrates used it. Fehleisen isolated the streptococcus in 1883.
erythema: From Greek erythros 'red.' The stem hides inside the names of many rashes.
euvolemia: From the Greek eu-, 'well.' The clinician's Goldilocks call.
fibrosis: The Latin fibra, 'thread.' What replaces working tissue when the body gives up on healing.
fungemia: The rise of ICU-level central lines in the 1970s made this a household word in hospital microbiology.
gliadin: Isolated from wheat by the Italian chemist Beccari in 1728 — two centuries before it was linked to a small-bowel disease.
hordeolum: The Latin for a barleycorn — the measure the ancient physicians used for the little lump at the lash-line.
hyperaldosteronism: An overshooting mineralocorticoid. The eponym belongs to the American endocrinologist who described the primary adrenal form in 1955.
kwashiorkor: Cicely Williams, a Jamaican-born pediatrician on the Gold Coast in the 1930s, adopted the local Ga name for the syndrome she was writing up in the Lancet.
laryngomalacia: From the Greek for 'softening.' The most common cause of noisy breathing in babies; the cartilage stiffens on its own by the second birthday.
lassa: In 1969 a Nigerian mission nurse fell ill; two Yale researchers who tried to isolate the virus died before it was named for her town.
lymphedema: Milroy described the hereditary form in 1892; the sporadic post-surgical form is far more common today.
melanoma: From Greek melas 'black.' The one skin cancer that has killed nobles since the Renaissance.
molluscum: The Latin diminutive of mollis, 'soft.' Small pearls with a dimple; children swap them at the pool.
mucocele: Greek mucus plus kele 'tumor.' The blue-tinged blister after a minor lip bite.
muscular: The Latin diminutive of mus, 'a little mouse' — because the twitching bicep looked like one darting under the skin.
nosocomial: Semmelweis in Vienna published his data on it in 1861. He was ignored, and died in an asylum four years later.
oligodendroglioma: Bailey and Cushing named it in the 1920s for a cell type the Spanish neuroanatomist Río Hortega had just described.
orthopnea: Greek orthos 'upright' plus pnoia 'breath.' A bedside test that predates the scan by two millennia.
parkinsonism: The 'Essay on the Shaking Palsy' was published in London in 1817 by the physician whose name now covers the whole syndrome.
peliosis: Wenzel illustrated the blood-filled hepatic cavities in 1861. They returned to the literature in the 1980s when bodybuilders and AIDS patients began dying with them.
periodontitis: The Egyptians treated it with honey and incense. The teeth are still there; what anchors them is not.
petechiae: Once called 'purple fever spots.' In eighteenth-century Naples, this was one of the classical findings that told a physician which epidemic was in town.
phthisis: Hippocrates named it 'wasting.' The bacillus behind it was not identified until Koch's 1882 lecture at the Berlin Physiological Society.
plague: Rodents, fleas, buboes, and a nursery rhyme.
polyneuritis: Greek poly- 'many' plus neur- 'nerve' plus -itis 'inflammation.' Bilateral, symmetric, and ascending.
polyneuropathy: The 'stocking-and-glove' distribution has been recognized since the nineteenth century as the calling card of diabetes, alcohol, and lead.
pyoderma: Greek pyon 'pus' plus derma 'skin.' Also names a striking ulcerative autoimmune disorder that responds to steroids, not antibiotics.
rickets: Common enough in seventeenth-century England that Francis Glisson wrote the first monograph on it in 1650. Cod-liver oil eventually solved most of it.
sarcopenia: The word was coined only in 1988 by an American nutrition researcher, from the Greek for 'poverty of flesh.'
sialadenitis: Before there was a vaccine, this was the childhood swelling that put a wedge between the ear and the jaw.
strangulated: The adjective from the Latin for choking. Turns an ordinary hernia from urgent to emergent.
takayasu: The Japanese ophthalmologist Mikito Takayasu described the arteritis in 1908 by noticing wreaths of new vessels around the optic nerves of his young female patients.
takotsubo: A Japanese pot for catching octopus. Grief kills.
thromboembolism: Virchow's 1850s triad — stasis, endothelial injury, hypercoagulability — was proposed to explain how a clot in one vein could kill a patient elsewhere.
tracheomalacia: From Greek malakia, 'softness.' The cartilaginous rings that hold the airway open are too pliant.
ureteric: The Greeks named the ureter from the verb 'to make water.' The adjective often carries 'stone' or 'reflux' behind it.
vaginitis: The Romans called it the fluor albus.
varicose: Roman legionaries had them burnt out with a hot iron. Aulus Cornelius Celsus described the surgery in the first century CE.
varicose veins: Roman legionaries had them burnt out with a hot iron. Aulus Cornelius Celsus described the surgery in the first century CE.
volvulus: From the Latin volvere, 'to turn.' On a plain abdominal film it draws the shape of a coffee bean.
vulvovaginitis: In the eighteenth century it went by 'the whites.' Estrogen deficiency underlies most modern cases.
whipple: The American surgeon lent his name to a pancreatic-head resection in 1935; separately, in 1907, he had described a bacterial malabsorption disease he found at autopsy.
whooping: The inspiratory sound is the reason for the vernacular English name. The P in DTaP was added to prevent it.
liver failure: Hepatic encephalopathy, coagulopathy, and jaundice — the classical Trey and Davidson triad from 1970 that defines the failing organ.
herpes simplex: The Greek 'creeping,' for the way the lesion spreads across mucosa. Sir William Osler wrote entire chapters on it before the virus was isolated in 1919.
nephrolithiasis: Hippocrates named the stone-cutters' craft in the Oath and swore against it. Millennia later, extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy replaced the knife.
alzheimers: The Bavarian psychiatrist Alois Alzheimer presented Auguste Deter's case at a Tübingen conference in 1906. His mentor Kraepelin gave the disease his name.
urinary tract infection: Egyptian medical papyri from 1550 BCE describe the burning sensation. Modern antibiotics resolve most in three days.
carbuncle: The Latin carbunculus, a small ember — for the fiery red pit the abscess leaves behind. Once fatal in the pre-antibiotic era.
borderline personality disorder: Adolph Stern coined the term in 1938 for patients he thought sat on the border of neurosis and psychosis. The DSM-III formalized it in 1980.
glomerulonephritis: Bright's disease, in nineteenth-century terms, when Richard Bright at Guy's Hospital linked the swollen faces and dark urine to inflamed renal glomeruli.
small bowel obstruction: The plain film shows air-fluid levels stepping across the abdomen like a ladder. Adhesions from prior surgery are the leading modern cause.
dementia: The Latin de- 'without' plus mens 'mind.' Philippe Pinel used it in 1801 to describe the chronic loss of reasoning distinct from acute delirium.
thalassemia: The Greek thalassa, 'sea,' for the Mediterranean populations Cooley first described in 1925. Two Detroit pediatricians named it after the geography of its heritable pool.
tuberculosis: Robert Koch's 1882 Berlin lecture identified the bacillus that had killed one in seven Europeans of the preceding century.
duodenal ulcer: The stomach's downstream neighbor, twelve fingerbreadths long — the Latin duodenum. Warren and Marshall's Helicobacter discovery in 1982 rewrote the whole disease.
proteinuria: A fingerprint of the leaking glomerulus. Bright's early nineteenth-century test heated the urine over a candle to look for the tell-tale white cloud.
pcos: Stein and Leventhal described the syndrome in seven women in 1935 — enlarged, cystic ovaries with amenorrhea and hirsutism.
kawasaki: Tomisaku Kawasaki, a Japanese pediatrician, described the fever-with-strawberry-tongue syndrome in 1967. Coronary aneurysms are the feared sequela.
prediabetes: A twentieth-century designation for the metabolic middle ground — a fasting glucose that isn't yet diabetic but predicts it.
menorrhagia: The Greek 'burst forth monthly.' Once treated with hysterectomy; now with hormones, ablation, and tranexamic acid.
lymphoma: Thomas Hodgkin described the enlarged nodes at autopsy at Guy's Hospital in 1832. The eponymous variant is one of two big families.
narcolepsy: The French neurologist Gélineau coined the word in 1880 from Greek 'seized by sleep.' The hypocretin-neuron loss wasn't identified until 2000.
supraventricular tachycardia: A re-entrant loop above the ventricles fires the heart faster than 150. Vagal maneuvers or a bolus of adenosine can break it.
constipation: The Latin constipare, 'to press close together.' Its clinical study spans the Roman herbal writers through the modern gut motility labs.
retinoblastoma: The paradigmatic 'two-hit' cancer that gave Alfred Knudson his 1971 hypothesis. Leukocoria in a toddler's eye is the parental warning.
gerd: The valve at the top of the stomach loses tone, and acid climbs where it shouldn't. Named for the reflux pattern rather than the tissue it inflames.
tachycardia: The Greek tachy- 'swift' plus kardia 'heart.' Anything above 100 beats per minute at rest qualifies.
pleural effusion: Fluid pools in the space between lung and chest wall. Percussion goes dull; breath sounds fade; a diagnostic tap draws off the answer.
kidney stones: Documented in ancient Egyptian mummies. The pain was ranked by Renaissance surgeons as one of the three worst a human can suffer, alongside childbirth and gout.
tennis elbow: The nineteenth-century sports press named it. Any repetitive gripping motion — carpentry, plumbing, painting — inflames the same tendon.
neuroblastoma: A cancer of the sympathetic nervous chain in infants. Named by Wright in 1910 for its immature nerve-cell origin.
cirrhosis: The Greek kirrhos, 'tawny' — for the color of the scarred liver at autopsy. Laennec named it in 1819 alongside his stethoscope.
chest pain: The chief presenting complaint of both benign muscle strain and lethal aortic dissection. Ruling in and out is a first-year residency ritual.
endometriosis: John Sampson at Yale proposed retrograde menstruation as the mechanism in 1927. Diagnosis still requires laparoscopy a century later.
pneumothorax: The Greek 'wind in the chest.' The lung deflates, breath sounds vanish on one side, and a chest tube re-expands it.
cholera: John Snow removed the Broad Street pump handle in 1854 and mapped the outbreak — a founding moment of epidemiology.
bradycardia: Greek brady- 'slow' plus kardia 'heart.' Below 60 beats per minute at rest; athletes carry it as a badge, geriatric hearts as a warning.
low back pain: The most common cause of activity limitation in adults under 45. Mostly muscular and self-limiting; sometimes a herniated disc.
addisons: Thomas Addison at Guy's Hospital in 1855 described the bronzed skin, weight loss, and salt craving of the failing adrenal cortex.
esophageal varices: The portal system, dammed at the cirrhotic liver, backs up into the collateral veins at the base of the food-tube. They rupture catastrophically.
dysphagia: Greek dys- 'bad' plus phagein 'to eat.' The complaint that puts strictures, motility disorders, and tumors on the same differential.
deep vein thrombosis: Virchow's 1856 triad — stasis, hypercoagulability, endothelial injury — still explains why the calf swells after a long flight.
aortic aneurysm: The Greek aneurysma, 'a widening.' Andreas Vesalius illustrated one at autopsy in 1557; modern imaging catches most before they burst.
pharyngitis: The everyday sore throat. Most viral; a small minority strep, worth swabbing to prevent rheumatic fever.
emphysema: Laennec first heard the loss of elasticity with his stethoscope in 1819. Cigarettes made it a mass disease a century later.
aids: 1981 CDC morbidity reports of Pneumocystis and Kaposi's sarcoma in gay men in Los Angeles opened the epidemic. Antiretrovirals arrived in 1996.
sunburn: The oldest recorded skin injury; ancient Egyptians treated it with aloe. The wavelength that causes it — UVB — was pinned down in the 1920s.
migraine: Hippocrates called it 'hemikrania' — half the head. Modern triptans arrived only in the 1990s.
jaundice: The old French jaune, 'yellow.' Physicians distinguished the pre-hepatic, hepatic, and post-hepatic forms long before liver biochemistry.
copd: Sir William Briscoe first used the term in 1965. Smoking is the near-monopoly cause worldwide.
urticaria: The Latin urtica, 'nettle' — for the raised, itching welts that mimic a brush against the plant.
diabetes mellitus: The Greek 'to pass through' plus the Latin 'honey-sweet' — because ancient physicians tasted the urine to diagnose it. Willis added the mellitus in 1675.
atherosclerosis: Marchand's turn-of-the-century term for the porridge-like plaques (Greek athere, 'gruel') accumulating in artery walls.
whooping cough: The characteristic inspiratory whoop between paroxysms of dry cough. The vaccine that curbed it is the P in DTaP.
hearing loss: Presbycusis to the geriatrician; noise exposure to the audiologist; sensorineural or conductive to the otologist. All present as the same complaint.
hypocalcemia: Chvostek's sign taps the facial nerve; Trousseau's inflates a cuff to produce carpal spasm. Both come from the calcium-hungry motor nerves of the last century.
diarrhea: The Greek 'to flow through.' Rotavirus alone once killed half a million children a year globally; the vaccine is transforming that number.
hemorrhoids: The Greek 'blood flow.' Julius Caesar, Napoleon, and the Egyptian pharaohs all reportedly suffered them, according to physicians who wrote about them.
retinal detachment: Coats and Gonin worked out the pathology in the early twentieth century — the sensory retina peels away from the pigment epithelium. Surgical reattachment saves the sight.
trigeminal neuralgia: Fothergill described the shock-like face pain in 1773. Called 'tic douloureux' in nineteenth-century French — the painful tic.
hyperkalemia: A potassium above 5.5 turns the ECG waves peaked. Above 7, the heart may arrest. Calcium gluconate, insulin, and albuterol buy the time to dialyze.
cystitis: The Greek kystis, 'bladder,' plus -itis. The bane of postmenopausal women, honeymooners, and paraplegics alike.
bacteremia: Bacteria in the blood — a step less severe than sepsis, a step more than local infection. Blood cultures name the offender.
hidradenitis: The sweat glands of the axilla and groin block, inflame, and scar. Suppurativa is the worst-tempered variant, first described by Verneuil in 1854.
als: Charcot's 1874 description of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis. In the United States, Lou Gehrig's 1939 farewell speech attached his name to it.
obesity: The World Health Organization declared it a global epidemic in 1997. BMI as a screening tool dates only to 1972.
uveitis: Inflammation of the middle vascular layer of the eye. Reiter's syndrome, sarcoidosis, and juvenile idiopathic arthritis all present in the ophthalmologist's chair.
chlamydia: The most commonly reported bacterial STI in developed countries. The organism is neither classically bacterial nor viral — it needs a host cell to divide.
uterine cancer: The most common gynecologic malignancy in developed countries. Endometrial adenocarcinoma is the usual histology; obesity and unopposed estrogen are the drivers.
brain tumor: Primary intracranial masses run the gamut from meningiomas to gliomas. Cushing's surgical volumes in the 1920s established the modern neurosurgical approach.
anemia: The Greek 'without blood.' Once diagnosed by pallor and pica; now by a hemoglobin below the reference range.
fatigue: The Latin fatigare, 'to weary.' A symptom shared by anemia, hypothyroidism, depression, sleep apnea, and the human condition.
ascites: The Greek askos, 'wineskin.' Fluid fills the peritoneum; cirrhosis is the leading cause; a diagnostic tap distinguishes it from malignancy.
ibd: A twentieth-century umbrella for two chronic, relapsing inflammatory disorders of the gut — one that skips segments and one that starts at the rectum and marches up.
hernia: The Latin for a rupture or fissure. The Alexandrian surgeon Celsus described inguinal repair in the first century; Bassini modernized it in 1887.
graves disease: Robert James Graves at Dublin's Meath Hospital described the goiter, palpitations, and bulging eyes in 1835. The stimulating antibody wasn't identified until 1956.
ileus: The Latin for 'twisting.' Post-operative gut paralysis without mechanical obstruction; the bowel just stops moving until it decides to resume.
hematochezia: The Greek 'bloody stool.' Bright red rectal bleeding — usually from below the ileocecal valve, most often diverticula or hemorrhoids.
